one_liner: A 9-year-old boy with a history of learning difficulties is evaluated for numerous cafe-au-lait spots, axillary freckling, and soft skin nodules.

stage2.hpi_extension: The mother noted several flat tan skin lesions at birth that have increased in size and number. Over the past two years, freckling in the armpits and groin has developed. In the last six months, he has complained of mild headaches and has been falling behind in school, with newly identified learning disabilities in reading and math. No seizures. No vision loss reported, but he has not had a formal eye exam.
stage2.pmh: ADHD. Recurrent headaches. Growth and motor milestones otherwise normal.
stage2.family_history: Mother has numerous cafe-au-lait spots and a few soft cutaneous nodules on her back; she was told she had 'NF' as a child. Maternal grandmother died of an 'adrenal tumor'.
stage2.social_history: Lives with mother and two siblings. 4th grade student receiving special education services.
stage2.physical_exam: General: well-appearing boy. Skin: >6 cafe-au-lait macules, each > 5 mm in diameter, predominantly on the trunk. Axillary and inguinal freckling (Crowe sign). Multiple soft, flesh-colored pedunculated cutaneous nodules (neurofibromas) and a larger, ropy plexiform neurofibroma over the right shoulder. HEENT: Lisch nodules (pigmented iris hamartomas) seen bilaterally on slit-lamp examination. Spine: mild thoracic scoliosis. Neuro: normal strength and gait; visual acuity grossly intact.

lab_results.result: NF1 gene sequencing reveals a heterozygous loss-of-function (truncating) mutation in the NF1 gene on chromosome 17q11.2, consistent with neurofibromatosis type 1.
lab_results.clinical_significance: NF1 encodes neurofibromin, a tumor suppressor that negatively regulates Ras (GTPase-activating protein). Loss-of-function mutations cause increased Ras signaling and predisposition to neural crest-derived tumors. AD inheritance with 100% penetrance but variable expressivity.

lab_results.result: MRI brain and orbits with contrast: enhancing lesion of the left optic nerve consistent with optic pathway glioma. Multiple T2-hyperintense foci in the basal ganglia and brainstem (unidentified bright objects / UBOs), which are characteristic but benign in NF1.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Optic pathway glioma is the most common CNS tumor in NF1 and can threaten vision. UBOs are benign MRI findings helpful in supporting the diagnosis.

lab_results.result: Biopsy of a dermal nodule: loosely arranged spindle cells in a myxoid matrix with wavy serpentine nuclei, consistent with a benign neurofibroma (S100 positive).
lab_results.clinical_significance: Benign cutaneous neurofibromas are one of the NIH diagnostic criteria for NF1. Plexiform neurofibromas carry an increased risk of transformation to malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor (MPNST).

stage5.final_clue: Ongoing surveillance includes annual ophthalmology (Lisch nodules and optic glioma), blood pressure monitoring (pheochromocytoma and renal artery stenosis), spine exams for scoliosis, developmental assessment, and dermatologic surveillance for new or changing plexiform neurofibromas (risk of transformation to MPNST). The mother is referred for adult NF1 care, including surveillance for breast cancer.

debrief.classic_presentation: NF1 classically presents in childhood with multiple cafe-au-lait macules, axillary and inguinal freckling (Crowe sign), Lisch nodules (pigmented iris hamartomas), cutaneous and plexiform neurofibromas, optic pathway glioma, scoliosis, and learning disabilities. The NIH diagnostic criteria require 2 of 7 features including 6 cafe-au-lait spots, freckling in skin folds, 2 neurofibromas or 1 plexiform, optic glioma, 2 Lisch nodules, a distinctive bony lesion, or a first-degree relative with NF1.
debrief.pathophysiology: NF1 is an autosomal dominant disorder caused by loss-of-function mutations in the NF1 gene on chromosome 17q11.2, which encodes neurofibromin, a GTPase-activating protein (GAP) that negatively regulates Ras. Loss of neurofibromin leads to unopposed Ras signaling and tumor formation in neural crest derivatives. NF1 has 100% penetrance but highly variable expressivity and one of the highest spontaneous mutation rates in the genome, with ~50% of cases arising de novo.
debrief.treatment_overview: No curative therapy exists; management is multidisciplinary surveillance: annual ophthalmology (Lisch nodules, optic glioma), serial imaging for symptomatic CNS lesions, blood pressure monitoring (screen for pheochromocytoma and renal artery stenosis), orthopedic follow-up for scoliosis and tibial pseudoarthrosis, developmental support, and dermatologic evaluation of changing plexiform neurofibromas (concern for malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor). MEK inhibitors (e.g., selumetinib) can shrink plexiform neurofibromas, but this is more recent than First Aid 2017.
high_yield_pearls: NF1 = AD, NF1 gene (chromosome 17), neurofibromin -> negative regulator of Ras (tumor suppressor).
Diagnostic features: cafe-au-lait spots, axillary/inguinal freckling (Crowe sign), Lisch nodules, neurofibromas, optic glioma.
Remember chromosome 17 for NF1 (17 letters in 'von Recklinghausen'); NF2 is on chromosome 22.
Increased risk of optic glioma, pheochromocytoma, MPNST, and learning disabilities.
Plexiform neurofibromas can transform to malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumors (MPNST) — watch for rapid growth or pain.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Neurology and Special Senses chapter, 'Neurocutaneous disorders — Neurofibromatosis type 1' section.
First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Biochemistry chapter, 'Autosomal dominant diseases' (NF1, neurofibromin).
